answer.rationale: Apparatus + interlinear converge on God's unrestricted power: the Niphal of pala (H6381) negated in Jer 32:17 and adynateo (G101) negated in Luke 1:37 both lexically express 'nothing impossible'. The concordance for these lemmas across the canon shows pan-canonical demonstration; anthropopathism passages (Gen 6:6, Jonah 3:10) require accommodation reading and do not refute.
answer.notes: Divine omnipotence is gospel-essential under cross-tradition consensus, but cult-marker fails the corrected bar: Open Theism and Process Theology dissent are not pan-tradition rejected (some traditions tolerate the dissent, e.g. Methodist openness debate). would_die_for=true reflects gospel weight; cult_marker=false reflects that denial is heterodox-but-not-cult.

lay_summary.reasoning: Scripture teaches that God is all-powerful and able to do all that he wills, limited only by his own nature. The clearest passages are Genesis 17:1 where God reveals himself as El Shaddai, Job 42:2 where Job confesses God can do all things and no purpose of his can be thwarted, Jeremiah 32:17 where the prophet says nothing is too hard for God, and Matthew 19:26 where Jesus says with God all things are possible. The strongest counterargument cites passages where God appears to repent or change course, such as Genesis 6:6 where the LORD is sorry he made humankind, Exodus 32:14 where the LORD changes his mind about the calamity, 1 Samuel 15:11 where the LORD regrets making Saul king, and Jonah 3:10 where God relents from the disaster he had said he would do. The verdict handles this tension by reading these passages as accommodation language describing God in human terms so finite minds can understand his real interaction with creatures.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: All eight major historic Christian lineages affirm divine omnipotence as a core attribute of God: patristic teaching from Athanasius onward, Catholic magisterial teaching, Lutheran confessions, Anglican formularies, Reformed standards, Methodist articles, Anabaptist tradition, and Pentecostal statements all confess God as almighty. Open Theism (Greg Boyd, Clark Pinnock) is a minority position within evangelical theology that limits divine knowledge of future free actions but still affirms omnipotence proper. Process theology (Charles Hartshorne, John Cobb) denies classical omnipotence in favor of a persuasive rather than coercive deity; this position is held by some mainline scholars but is outside historic Christian confession. Mormonism's eternal-progression doctrine implies God is one being among many and was once like humanity; this places Mormonism outside historic Christianity on this point.

scripture.key_term: לֹא יִפָּלֵא (lo yippale) — Niphal of pala, H6381
scripture.force: pala (Niphal) = 'to be surpassing, extraordinary, beyond power'; negated: 'nothing is too extraordinary for You'
adynateo = 'to be impossible'; future negated: 'nothing will be impossible with God' (para Theou)
Niphal of nacham = 'to be sorry / regret'; literal reading would imply mutability, but the form is anthropopathic accommodation language for divine response to human moral state

scripture.key_term: וַיִּנָּחֶם הָאֱלֹהִים — same Niphal of nacham, H5162
scripture.force: God 'relents' from declared judgment; same anthropopathic figure as Gen 6:6, conditional-prophecy framework softens the apparent mutability

hermeneutics.notes: Anthropopathic passages handled by accommodation principle (Calvin's 'lisps'); pan-canonical concordance of pala (H6381) and adynateo (G101) supports the unrestricted-power reading; nacham (H5162) occurrences across the canon show the lemma is regularly used in conditional-prophecy contexts where literal mutability is not in view.

counter_witness.key_phrase: He is called Almighty because He does whatever He wills. Although what some men will to do, He, with even greater might, prevents.
Of all the divine attributes, only God's omnipotence is named in the Creed: to confess this power has great bearing on our lives. We believe that it is universal.
There is one divine essence... eternal, without body, indivisible, of infinite power, wisdom, and goodness, the Maker and Preserver of all things.
almighty, most wise, most holy, most free, most absolute, working all things according to the counsel of His own immutable and most righteous will

web.quote: לֹא־יִפָּלֵא — pala Niphal yiqtol 3ms 'will not be too difficult'
Catechism §268: God's omnipotence is universal, loving, and mysterious
